victim.name: Lord Alistair Finch
victim.background: Known for ruthless business practices, recently acquired a highly controversial and lucrative patent, making him many enemies.

culprit.name: Eleanor Vance
culprit.means: Access to Lord Finch's study and a heavy silver letter opener from his desk.
culprit.motive: Lord Finch stole her family's intellectual property years ago, built his empire on it, and recently fired her father, leading to his ruin. She discovered proof of the theft.
culprit.opportunity: She was working late in her adjacent office, and everyone else had verifiable alibis or was occupied elsewhere during the time of the murder.
culprit.method: Struck Lord Finch from behind with a heavy silver letter opener.
culprit.alibi: Claims she was in her office, preparing documents for an early morning meeting, and heard nothing due to the severe storm outside. This is plausible but false.

suspects.name: Lady Beatrice Finch
suspects.means: Yes, physically capable and had full access to the manor.
suspects.motive: Yes, Lord Finch was having a public affair, and she stood to inherit a vast fortune upon his death.
suspects.alibi: I was playing bridge with Mrs. Albright and Colonel Davies in the drawing-room from 9 PM until midnight. We never left the room. (Verifiable by Mrs. Albright and Colonel Davies).
suspects.personality: Cold, aristocratic, outwardly composed but inwardly seething with resentment.

suspects.name: Dr. Elias Thorne
suspects.relationship_to_victim: Long-time family doctor and a close friend of Lord Finch's late brother.
suspects.means: Yes, physically capable and had access to the manor as he was making a house call earlier.
suspects.opportunity: Yes, he was in the manor, having made a house call earlier in the evening and decided to stay to read.
suspects.alibi: I was in the library, reading and enjoying a brandy, from 9:30 PM onwards. I saw no one enter or leave the study wing. (Verifiable by the half-empty brandy glass and a bookmark in a specific book).
suspects.personality: Calm, intellectual, observant, a bit melancholic, and genuinely fond of Lord Finch despite his flaws.

suspects.name: Thomas "Tom" O'Malley
suspects.relationship_to_victim: Long-serving employee, recently had a heated argument with Lord Finch over his pension.
suspects.means: Yes, strong and had access to various tools and the manor's grounds.
suspects.motive: Yes, Lord Finch threatened to cut his pension after a minor incident, causing significant financial distress.
suspects.alibi: I was out in the storm, securing the greenhouses and checking the perimeter fences. Young Billy (the junior groundskeeper) was with me for most of it, until he went to bed around 10:45 PM. I came in around 11:15 PM, soaked. (Verifiable by Billy and the state of the greenhouses).
suspects.personality: Gruff, loyal to the estate, but easily angered when provoked and fiercely protective of his livelihood.

clues.description: A distinctive silver button, slightly tarnished, found near the victim's body.
clues.location: Lord Finch's Study, on the rug near the desk.

clues.description: A torn piece of a love letter, partially burned, mentioning 'our secret rendezvous' and 'escaping this gilded cage'.
clues.location: Lord Finch's Study, in the fireplace.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Lady Finch was indeed having an affair, but with a different man. She was burning old letters to cover her tracks, not because she killed Alistair. The affair was known to Alistair, but not the identity of the lover.

clues.description: A small, almost invisible, smear of a specific chemical compound found on the silver button (Clue 01) after laboratory analysis.
Muddy boot prints, large in size, leading from the study's back door towards the garden.
clues.location: Back corridor leading to the garden, near the study.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Thomas did use that door earlier in the evening to bring in some potted plants, and the storm made the ground muddy. The prints are old, not fresh from the time of death, but appear suspicious.

clues.description: A hidden compartment in Eleanor Vance's desk containing old family documents and a detailed, hand-drawn diagram of a chemical formula identical to Lord Finch's 'new' patent.
A faint, metallic smell on Eleanor Vance's uniform jacket, specifically near where the missing button (Clue 01) would have been.
clues.location: Eleanor Vance's Office, her uniform jacket hanging on a coat rack.

timeline.event: Lady Finch, Mrs. Albright, and Colonel Davies begin their bridge game in the drawing-room.
Dr. Thorne settles in the library with a book and brandy.
Eleanor Vance finishes her 'work' in her office and proceeds to Lord Finch's study.
Thomas O'Malley and Billy secure greenhouses. Thomas makes a quick trip inside through the back door to grab something, leaving muddy prints.
Eleanor Vance confronts Lord Finch in his study. An argument ensues.
Lord Finch is struck and killed by Eleanor Vance. She loses a button from her jacket in the struggle.
Eleanor Vance returns to her office, feigning work.
Thomas O'Malley returns inside, soaked, and goes to his quarters.
A housemaid discovers Lord Finch's body in his study.

hidden_backstory: Eleanor Vance, Lord Finch's seemingly loyal secretary, harbored a deep-seated grudge. Years ago, Lord Finch had stolen a groundbreaking chemical formula from her father, using it to build his vast industrial empire while her family was ruined. Recently, Eleanor discovered irrefutable proof of this theft among her father's old papers and realized Lord Finch was about to patent a new iteration of the same stolen technology, further cementing his ill-gotten gains. On the night of the murder, she confronted him in his study, demanding justice for her family. When Lord Finch scoffed at her, dismissing her claims and threatening her, Eleanor, in a fit of rage and desperation, grabbed a heavy silver letter opener from his desk and struck him from behind, killing him instantly. She then quickly returned to her office, feigning work, hoping the storm and the manor's isolated nature would provide cover for her crime.
